subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look carefully at the world around you. The iron in a farmer's hoe near the Jubba River, the gold dug from mines in the East African highlands, the carbon in a bag of charcoal, the oxygen you are breathing in this very moment, the salt on your family's table, and the calcium in your own bones and in the limestone rocks along the coast - every single one of these is built from tiny building blocks called atoms. In this unit you will discover that the entire universe, from the smallest grain of sand to the largest star, is made from just over one hundred basic substances called elements.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 8 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain what an atom is and describe it as the smallest part of an element.
Define an element and explain why there are only around 118 of them.
Read and write chemical symbols correctly, using capital and lowercase letters properly.
Describe how the Periodic Table is arranged into groups (columns) and periods (rows).
Tell metals apart from non-metals by their properties, with local examples of each.
Describe the properties of some important groups, such as Group 1, Group 7 and Group 0.
Explain the difference between an element, a compound and a mixture.
Read and understand simple chemical formulae such as H2O, CO2 and NaCl.
Describe what happens in a chemical reaction, using the words reactant and product.

concepts.title: Elements and the Periodic Table — part 1
concepts.explanation: Look carefully at the world around you. The iron in a farmer's hoe near the Jubba River, the gold dug from mines in the East African highlands, the carbon in a bag of charcoal, the oxygen you are breathing in this very moment, the salt on your family's table, and the calcium in your own bones and in the limestone rocks along the coast - every single one of these is built from tiny building blocks called atoms. In this unit you will discover that the entire universe, from the smallest grain of sand to the largest…
concepts.example: This is one of the most powerful ideas in all of science. For thousands of years people believed there were countless different materials with nothing in common. Then, step by step, scientists discovered that everything…

concepts.title: Elements and the Periodic Table — part 2
concepts.explanation: This is one of the most powerful ideas in all of science. For thousands of years people believed there were countless different materials with nothing in common. Then, step by step, scientists discovered that everything is made from a surprisingly small set of pure substances. Even more astonishing, in the year 1869 a Russian scientist named Dmitri Mendeleev found a way to arrange all the known elements into a single, beautiful chart - the Periodic Table. This chart is so clever that it not only organised the…
concepts.example: By the end of this unit you will understand what an atom is, what makes an element different from a compound, how to read the chemical symbols that scientists use as a worldwide shorthand, how the Periodic Table is…

concepts.title: Elements and the Periodic Table — part 3
concepts.explanation: By the end of this unit you will understand what an atom is, what makes an element different from a compound, how to read the chemical symbols that scientists use as a worldwide shorthand, how the Periodic Table is arranged into groups and periods, and why metals behave so differently from non-metals. You will also take your first steps into chemical reactions - the changes that turn one substance into a completely new one, such as iron turning to rust or charcoal burning to give warmth. This knowledge sits behind…
concepts.example: Study this table before you read on. These words appear again and again throughout the unit, so learning them early will make everything that follows much easier.

concepts.explanation: A pure substance made of only one type of atom; it cannot be broken into simpler substances.

concepts.explanation: An element that is usually shiny, conducts heat and electricity, and can be bent or hammered.
concepts.example: Look carefully at the world around you. The iron in a farmer's hoe near the Jubba River, the gold dug from mines in the East African highlands, the carbon in a bag of charcoal, the oxygen you are breathing in this very…

explorations.title: Sorting Materials into Metals and Non-metals
explorations.context: To collect objects from around your home and sort them into metals and non-metals by testing their properties.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down: Which single test do you think will be the best for telling a metal from a non-metal - shininess, hardness, or the sound it makes when tapped? Explain your guess.
explorations.answer: Which objects were easiest to sort? Which were hardest, and why? Was your hypothesis correct - which test turned out to be the most reliable? The piece of charcoal is a non-metal made of the element carbon. Which of its properties told you it was a non-metal?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To collect objects from around your home and sort them into metals and non-metals by testing their properties.

explorations.title: Testing for Metals with a Magnet
explorations.context: To find out which metals in your home are magnetic, and to learn that being magnetic is a special property of only a few metals, not all of them.
explorations.prompt: Write down: Do you think all shiny metal objects will be attracted to a magnet? Explain your prediction before you test.
explorations.answer: Were all the metals attracted to the magnet? What does this tell you about the property of being magnetic? The iron nail is attracted but the aluminium can is not, even though both are metals. What does this show? Were any non-metals attracted to the magnet? Why do you think that is?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which metals in your home are magnetic, and to learn that being magnetic is a special property of only a few metals, not all of them.

explorations.title: A Chemical Reaction with Vinegar and Limestone
explorations.context: To observe a chemical reaction between an acid (vinegar) and a compound containing calcium (chalk, limestone, or an eggshell), and to spot the signs that a reaction has taken place.
explorations.prompt: Write down: What do you think will happen when vinegar touches the eggshell or chalk? Will you see any signs of a chemical reaction? Predict before you start.
explorations.answer: The bubbles are a gas (carbon dioxide) being made. Which sign of a chemical reaction does this show? The eggshell slowly disappears. Explain why this shows a new substance is being made, not just mixing. Name the two reactants in this reaction, and name one product you observed.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe a chemical reaction between an acid (vinegar) and a compound containing calcium (chalk, limestone, or an eggshell), and to spot the signs that a reaction has taken place.

explorations.title: Watching Rust Form (A Slow Chemical Reaction)
explorations.context: To observe the slow chemical reaction of rusting, and to find out what conditions make iron rust faster.
explorations.prompt: Write down: What do you think iron needs in order to rust - just air, just water, or both together? Make a prediction before you set up the experiment.
explorations.answer: Which nail rusted the most? What does this tell you about what iron needs to rust? Rusting is a chemical reaction between iron and oxygen. Why did the nail with both air and water rust fastest? How does this experiment explain why we oil or paint metal tools and gates?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe the slow chemical reaction of rusting, and to find out what conditions make iron rust faster.

methods.title: Sorting Materials into Metals and Non-metals
methods.example: To collect objects from around your home and sort them into metals and non-metals by testing their properties.
steps: Draw a table with two columns: Metal and Non-metal.
Take each object in turn. Look at it - is it shiny or dull?
Gently tap it against a hard surface - does it make a ringing sound or a dull thud?
Feel it - is it cold and hard (many metals feel cold) or not?
Decide whether it is a metal or a non-metal, and write it in the correct column with your reason.

methods.title: Testing for Metals with a Magnet
methods.example: To find out which metals in your home are magnetic, and to learn that being magnetic is a special property of only a few metals, not all of them.
steps: Lay out all your objects on a table.
Bring the magnet slowly near each object in turn.
Notice whether the object is attracted (pulled towards the magnet) or not attracted.
Record your result for each object.
Sort your results into two groups: magnetic and not magnetic.

methods.title: A Chemical Reaction with Vinegar and Limestone
methods.example: To observe a chemical reaction between an acid (vinegar) and a compound containing calcium (chalk, limestone, or an eggshell), and to spot the signs that a reaction has taken place.
steps: Place the crushed eggshell or piece of chalk in the clear glass.
Pour a little vinegar over it, just enough to cover it.
Watch very closely for the first few minutes. Look for bubbles or fizzing.
Listen carefully - can you hear a soft fizzing sound?
Record everything you observe, and note how long the fizzing lasts.

methods.title: Watching Rust Form (A Slow Chemical Reaction)
methods.example: To observe the slow chemical reaction of rusting, and to find out what conditions make iron rust faster.
steps: Put one nail in a dry jar with nothing else. This nail has air but no water.
Put the second nail in a jar and cover it completely with water, then add a thin layer of oil on top to keep air out. This nail has water but little air.
Put the third nail in a jar with just enough water to half-cover it, so it has both air and water.
Leave all three jars in a safe place and look at them once a day for four or five days.
Each day, record which nails are rusting and how much.

workedExamples.title: Counting Atoms in a Formula
workedExamples.prompt: Learning to count the atoms in a formula is one of the most useful skills in this unit. Let us work through some examples together, showing every step, so you can see exactly how it is done. Remember the two rules: a symbol with no number means one atom, and…
workedExamples.solution: Example 1: How many atoms are in H2O (water)? Look at each symbol in turn. The first is H (hydrogen), and just after it is a small 2. So there are 2 hydrogen atoms. The next symbol is O (oxygen), with no number after it. So there is 1 oxygen atom. Add them up: 2 + 1 = 3 atoms in total, made of 2 elements.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: About how many different elements are there? (a) 12 (b) 50 (c) 118 (d) 1000
workedExamples.solution: (c) 118. There are about 118 known elements, around 90 of them found naturally.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: The chemical symbol for iron is: (a) Ir (b) In (c) Fe (d) Fr
workedExamples.solution: (c) Fe. The symbol for iron comes from the Latin word ferrum.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: The chemical symbol for sodium is: (a) So (b) Sd (c) Na (d) S
workedExamples.solution: (c) Na. The symbol for sodium comes from the Latin word natrium.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: A vertical column in the Periodic Table is called a: (a) period (b) group (c) row (d) kingdom
workedExamples.solution: (b) group. A group is a vertical column; a period is a horizontal row.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a property of most metals? (a) dull (b) brittle (c) good conductor of electricity (d) a gas
workedExamples.solution: (c) good conductor of electricity. Metals conduct electricity well; the other options describe non-metals.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a non-metal? (a) iron (b) copper (c) oxygen (d) gold
workedExamples.solution: (c) oxygen. Oxygen is a non-metal gas. Iron, copper and gold are all metals.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Water, H2O, is an example of a: (a) element (b) mixture (c) compound (d) metal
workedExamples.solution: (c) compound. Water is two elements (hydrogen and oxygen) chemically joined, so it is a compound.

practice.prompt: The smallest particle of an element is called an: (a) molecule (b) atom (c) compound (d) mixture
practice.answer: (b) atom. An atom is the smallest particle of an element that still behaves as that element.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: About how many different elements are there? (a) 12 (b) 50 (c) 118 (d) 1000
practice.answer: (c) 118. There are about 118 known elements, around 90 of them found naturally.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The chemical symbol for iron is: (a) Ir (b) In (c) Fe (d) Fr
practice.answer: (c) Fe. The symbol for iron comes from the Latin word ferrum.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The chemical symbol for sodium is: (a) So (b) Sd (c) Na (d) S
practice.answer: (c) Na. The symbol for sodium comes from the Latin word natrium.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A vertical column in the Periodic Table is called a: (a) period (b) group (c) row (d) kingdom
practice.answer: (b) group. A group is a vertical column; a period is a horizontal row.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a property of most metals? (a) dull (b) brittle (c) good conductor of electricity (d) a gas
practice.answer: (c) good conductor of electricity. Metals conduct electricity well; the other options describe non-metals.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a non-metal? (a) iron (b) copper (c) oxygen (d) gold
practice.answer: (c) oxygen. Oxygen is a non-metal gas. Iron, copper and gold are all metals.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Water, H2O, is an example of a: (a) element (b) mixture (c) compound (d) metal
practice.answer: (c) compound. Water is two elements (hydrogen and oxygen) chemically joined, so it is a compound.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: How many atoms are in a molecule of CO2? (a) 1 (b) 2 (c) 3 (d) 4
practice.answer: (c) 3. CO2 has 1 carbon atom and 2 oxygen atoms, making 3 atoms in total.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In the reaction iron + oxygen -> rust, the rust is the: (a) reactant (b) product (c) element (d) mixture
practice.answer: (b) product. The rust is the new substance made, so it is the product; iron and oxygen are the reactants.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which group contains the very unreactive noble gases? (a) Group 1 (b) Group 7 (c) Group 0 (d) Group 3
practice.answer: (c) Group 0. The noble gases in Group 0 are very unreactive.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Common salt, NaCl, is made from the elements: (a) sodium and carbon (b) sodium and chlorine (c) nitrogen and chlorine (d) sodium and calcium
practice.answer: (b) sodium and chlorine. NaCl is made of sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl).
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Sorting Materials into Metals and Non-metals
activities.materials: A notebook and pencil; About ten safe objects: a metal spoon, a coin, an aluminium can, a copper wire, a piece of charcoal, a plastic cup, a wooden spoon, a stone, a piece of chalk or limestone, a rubber band
steps: Draw a table with two columns: Metal and Non-metal.
Take each object in turn. Look at it - is it shiny or dull?
Gently tap it against a hard surface - does it make a ringing sound or a dull thud?
Feel it - is it cold and hard (many metals feel cold) or not?
Decide whether it is a metal or a non-metal, and write it in the correct column with your reason.

activities.title: Testing for Metals with a Magnet
activities.materials: A magnet (a fridge magnet works well); A collection of metal and non-metal objects: an iron nail, a steel spoon, an aluminium can, a copper coin or wire, a gold or brass item if you have one, a plastic item, a piece of wood
steps: Lay out all your objects on a table.
Bring the magnet slowly near each object in turn.
Notice whether the object is attracted (pulled towards the magnet) or not attracted.
Record your result for each object.
Sort your results into two groups: magnetic and not magnetic.

activities.title: A Chemical Reaction with Vinegar and Limestone
activities.materials: A small amount of vinegar (a household acid); A clean, crushed eggshell, or a small piece of chalk or limestone (all contain calcium carbonate, CaCO3); A clear glass or jar; A spoon
steps: Place the crushed eggshell or piece of chalk in the clear glass.
Pour a little vinegar over it, just enough to cover it.
Watch very closely for the first few minutes. Look for bubbles or fizzing.
Listen carefully - can you hear a soft fizzing sound?
Record everything you observe, and note how long the fizzing lasts.

activities.title: Watching Rust Form (A Slow Chemical Reaction)
activities.materials: Three similar iron nails (steel nails work well); Three clear jars or cups; Water, and a little cooking oil; A notebook to record daily observations over several days
steps: Put one nail in a dry jar with nothing else. This nail has air but no water.
Put the second nail in a jar and cover it completely with water, then add a thin layer of oil on top to keep air out. This nail has water but little air.
Put the third nail in a jar with just enough water to half-cover it, so it has both air and water.
Leave all three jars in a safe place and look at them once a day for four or five days.
Each day, record which nails are rusting and how much.

activities.title: Observe: Elements and the Periodic Table — part 5
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of elements and the periodic table — part 5 around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Elements and the Periodic Table — part 6
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of elements and the periodic table — part 6 around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: An element that is shiny, conductive, malleable and strong.
An element that is dull, brittle and a poor conductor.
Two or more elements chemically joined into a new substance.
Substances mixed but not chemically joined.
Two or more atoms joined together.

vocabulary.example: Scientists describe a compound using a formula - a short code made of chemical symbols and small numbers.
Read and understand simple chemical formulae such as H2O, CO2 and NaCl.
Read and understand simple chemical formulae such as H2O, CO2 and NaCl.
Read and understand simple chemical formulae such as H2O, CO2 and NaCl.
CaCO3 (calcium carbonate, in limestone) means 1 calcium, 1 carbon and 3 oxygen atoms.
Gold, copper, sodium, calcium - each of these is a pure element with its own single type of atom.
O is oxygen, H is hydrogen, C is carbon, N is nitrogen, Ca is calcium, and Al is aluminium.

vocabulary.meaning: An element that is shiny, conductive, malleable and strong.
vocabulary.example: Yet every object you have ever touched - the camel's leather saddle, the metal cup, the water you drink, your own body - is built from countless billions upon billions of these tiny atoms.

vocabulary.meaning: An element that is dull, brittle and a poor conductor.
vocabulary.example: This is why the handle of a good cooking pot is often plastic or wood - non-metal materials that do not carry the heat to your hand.

vocabulary.meaning: Two or more elements chemically joined into a new substance.
vocabulary.example: Explain the difference between an element, a compound and a mixture.

vocabulary.meaning: Substances mixed but not chemically joined.
vocabulary.example: Explain the difference between an element, a compound and a mixture.

vocabulary.meaning: Two or more atoms joined together.

commonMistakes: An atom is one particle; an element is a whole substance.
Many symbols come from Latin, not English.
In a compound elements are chemically joined.
Only iron, nickel and cobalt are strongly magnetic.
It is groups (columns), not periods, that behave alike.

fluency.prompt: The smallest particle of an element is called an: (a) molecule (b) atom (c) compound (d) mixture
fluency.answer: (b) atom. An atom is the smallest particle of an element that still behaves as that element.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) atom. An atom is the smallest particle of an element that still behaves as that element.

fluency.prompt: About how many different elements are there? (a) 12 (b) 50 (c) 118 (d) 1000
fluency.answer: (c) 118. There are about 118 known elements, around 90 of them found naturally.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) 118. There are about 118 known elements, around 90 of them found naturally.

fluency.errorFeedback: (c) Na. The symbol for sodium comes from the Latin word natrium.

fluency.prompt: A vertical column in the Periodic Table is called a: (a) period (b) group (c) row (d) kingdom

fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) group. A group is a vertical column; a period is a horizontal row.

fluency.prompt: Which of these is a property of most metals? (a) dull (b) brittle (c) good conductor of electricity (d) a gas
fluency.answer: (c) good conductor of electricity. Metals conduct electricity well; the other options describe non-metals.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) good conductor of electricity. Metals conduct electricity well; the other options describe non-metals.

fluency.prompt: Which of these is a non-metal? (a) iron (b) copper (c) oxygen (d) gold
fluency.answer: (c) oxygen. Oxygen is a non-metal gas. Iron, copper and gold are all metals.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) oxygen. Oxygen is a non-metal gas. Iron, copper and gold are all metals.

fluency.prompt: Water, H2O, is an example of a: (a) element (b) mixture (c) compound (d) metal
fluency.answer: (c) compound. Water is two elements (hydrogen and oxygen) chemically joined, so it is a compound.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) compound. Water is two elements (hydrogen and oxygen) chemically joined, so it is a compound.

fluency.prompt: How many atoms are in a molecule of CO2? (a) 1 (b) 2 (c) 3 (d) 4
fluency.answer: (c) 3. CO2 has 1 carbon atom and 2 oxygen atoms, making 3 atoms in total.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) 3. CO2 has 1 carbon atom and 2 oxygen atoms, making 3 atoms in total.

fluency.prompt: In the reaction iron + oxygen -> rust, the rust is the: (a) reactant (b) product (c) element (d) mixture
fluency.answer: (b) product. The rust is the new substance made, so it is the product; iron and oxygen are the reactants.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) product. The rust is the new substance made, so it is the product; iron and oxygen are the reactants.

fluency.prompt: Which group contains the very unreactive noble gases? (a) Group 1 (b) Group 7 (c) Group 0 (d) Group 3
fluency.answer: (c) Group 0. The noble gases in Group 0 are very unreactive.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) Group 0. The noble gases in Group 0 are very unreactive.

fluency.prompt: Common salt, NaCl, is made from the elements: (a) sodium and carbon (b) sodium and chlorine (c) nitrogen and chlorine (d) sodium and calcium
fluency.answer: (b) sodium and chlorine. NaCl is made of sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl).
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) sodium and chlorine. NaCl is made of sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl).

realProblems.prompt: A blacksmith near Kismaayo hammers a hot piece of iron into a hoe without it shattering. Which property of metals makes this possible, and what is the name for that property?
realProblems.answer: The metal can be hammered into shape without shattering because it is malleable. This property lets a blacksmith shape hot iron into tools.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The metal can be hammered into shape without shattering because it is malleable. This property lets a blacksmith shape hot iron into tools.

realProblems.prompt: Amina notices that the salt left behind when sea water dries on the coast can be separated from the water easily. Does this make sea water a compound or a mixture? Explain.
realProblems.answer: It is a mixture, because the salt and water are not chemically joined and can be separated easily (the water dries away and leaves the salt behind).
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: It is a mixture, because the salt and water are not chemically joined and can be separated easily (the water dries away and leaves the salt behind).

realProblems.prompt: A farmer's iron gate rusts faster on the side facing the sea than the side facing inland. Using what you know about rusting, suggest why.
realProblems.answer: Salt in the sea air speeds up rusting, so the side facing the sea meets more salty, moist air and rusts faster. Rusting needs iron, oxygen and water, and salt makes it happen more quickly.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Salt in the sea air speeds up rusting, so the side facing the sea meets more salty, moist air and rusts faster. Rusting needs iron, oxygen and water, and salt makes it happen more quickly.

realProblems.prompt: Table salt is safe to eat, yet it is made from a dangerous metal and a poisonous gas. Explain how this is possible, using the words element and compound.
realProblems.answer: Table salt is a compound (sodium chloride). The dangerous element sodium and the poisonous element chlorine are chemically joined, and the compound has completely new, safe properties - unlike the elements it is made from.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Table salt is a compound (sodium chloride). The dangerous element sodium and the poisonous element chlorine are chemically joined, and the compound has completely new, safe properties - unlike the elements it is made from.

realProblems.prompt: A cook lights charcoal to prepare food. Name the two reactants and the one product of the burning reaction, and say what useful thing the reaction gives off.
realProblems.answer: The reactants are carbon (in the charcoal) and oxygen (from the air); the product is carbon dioxide. The reaction also gives off heat (and light), which cooks the food.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The reactants are carbon (in the charcoal) and oxygen (from the air); the product is carbon dioxide. The reaction also gives off heat (and light), which cooks the food.

realProblems.prompt: Mendeleev left gaps in his Periodic Table for elements that had not yet been discovered, and predicted their properties. Explain why this is such a powerful example of how a good scientific pattern works.
realProblems.answer: Mendeleev's gaps show that a good pattern does more than organise what we already know - it can predict what we do not yet know. Because the elements he predicted were later found with the properties he described, it proved the pattern was real and deeply true about nature.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Mendeleev's gaps show that a good pattern does more than organise what we already know - it can predict what we do not yet know. Because the elements he predicted were later found with the properties he described, it proved the pattern was real and deeply true about nature.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between an atom and an element.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: An atom is the smallest single particle of an element. An element is a pure substance made of many atoms of the same kind. For example, iron is an element, and a single iron atom is an atom.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State three properties of metals and three properties of non-metals.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Oxygen = O; carbon = C; iron = Fe; gold = Au; sodium = Na.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a group and a period in the Periodic Table?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Metals: shiny, good conductors of heat and electricity, malleable (bendable), strong, high melting point (any three). Non-metals: dull, poor conductors (insulators), brittle, low melting point, often gases (any three).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between a compound and a mixture, giving one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A group is a vertical column of the Periodic Table, and elements in it behave alike. A period is a horizontal row, across which elements change from metal to non-metal.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: How many atoms of each element are in the formula CaCO3? How many atoms in total?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: In a compound the elements are chemically joined and make a new substance (e.g. water). In a mixture they are only mixed and keep their own properties (e.g. air).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Define the words reactant and product, using rusting as your example.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: CaCO3 has 1 calcium, 1 carbon and 3 oxygen atoms. That is 5 atoms in total.

questions.question: The smallest particle of an element is called an:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: atom. An atom is the smallest particle of an element that still behaves as that element.

questions.question: About how many different elements are there?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: 118. There are about 118 known elements, around 90 of them found naturally.

questions.question: The chemical symbol for iron is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Fe. The symbol for iron comes from the Latin word ferrum.

questions.question: The chemical symbol for sodium is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Na. The symbol for sodium comes from the Latin word natrium.

questions.question: A vertical column in the Periodic Table is called a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: group. A group is a vertical column; a period is a horizontal row.

questions.question: Which of these is a property of most metals?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: good conductor of electricity. Metals conduct electricity well; the other options describe non-metals.

questions.question: Which of these is a non-metal?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: oxygen. Oxygen is a non-metal gas. Iron, copper and gold are all metals.

questions.question: Water, H2O, is an example of a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: compound. Water is two elements (hydrogen and oxygen) chemically joined, so it is a compound.

questions.question: How many atoms are in a molecule of CO2?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: 3. CO2 has 1 carbon atom and 2 oxygen atoms, making 3 atoms in total.

questions.question: In the reaction iron + oxygen -> rust, the rust is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: product. The rust is the new substance made, so it is the product; iron and oxygen are the reactants.

questions.question: Which group contains the very unreactive noble gases?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Group 0. The noble gases in Group 0 are very unreactive.

questions.question: Common salt, NaCl, is made from the elements:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: sodium and chlorine. NaCl is made of sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl).

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can explain what an atom is and describe it as the smallest part of an element.
I can define an element and explain why there are only around 118 of them.
I can read and write chemical symbols correctly, using capital and lowercase letters properly.
I can describe how the Periodic Table is arranged into groups (columns) and periods (rows).
I can tell metals apart from non-metals by their properties, with local examples of each.
I can describe the properties of some important groups, such as Group 1, Group 7 and Group 0.
I can explain the difference between an element, a compound and a mixture.
